data.title: Hong Kong firefighter unions slam Ip Man producer Checkley Sin over blaze remarks
data.description:
Six labour unions with Hong Kong’s Fire Services Department have denounced banker turned film producer Checkley Sin Kwok-lam for comments about its chief’s handling of the Tai Po fire.
The unions banded together on Thursday to slam Sin’s comments about the department’s strategies for battling the city’s deadliest fire in decades. The blaze raged for about 43 hours, killing 161 people and displacing nearly 5,000 residents.
Sin, a former banker who went on to produce the award-winning kung fu film series Ip Man, took to social media on Monday to claim that several friends in the department had given him internal messages that accused Director of Fire Services Andy Yeung Yan-kin of mismanaging the blaze.
In a statement, the unions said the accusations were misleading and had damaged the department’s professional image.
Sin shared the internal messages without identifying their source.
The unions argued that the department deployed drones to detect and monitor the fire at the scene, but could not use them to snuff out the fire due to limited water pressure.
They also said that airlifted water bombs could not be deployed because the rotors of the helicopters would fuel the air current and exacerbate the fire, while platforms to support aerial ladders were too wide for the road at the scene.
The Post has contacted Sin for comment.

data.title: ‘Christmas under fire’: Ukraine, Russia report fatal air strikes
data.description:
Russian air attacks caused death and destruction across Ukraine, authorities in different regions reported on Thursday, with Russian authorities reporting similar Ukrainian attacks on their territory.
A Russian drone attack in the southwestern Odesa region on the Black Sea left one person dead and two injured. Damage was caused to port and energy infrastructure, the regional authorities said.
In the Kharkiv region bordering Russia in Ukraine’s northeast, one person died and 15 were injured.
And in the Chernihiv region to the north of Kyiv, two people died in a drone attack on Wednesday.
“The Chernihiv region marked Christmas under fire. Unfortunately, two people died and two more were injured,” the authorities reported on Thursday.
The Energy Ministry reported numerous emergency shutdowns and outages across the country following the Russian attacks.


Ukrainian air defences reported that Russian forces had attacked overnight with 131 drones, 106 of which had been rendered harmless, while there had been 22 strikes at 15 locations.
Ukrainian leaders accused Moscow of terrorism over Christmas, adding that power and heating outages during winter were particularly dangerous for many people.
In Moscow, the Russian Defence Ministry reported downing more than 100 Ukrainian drones.
Sergey Melikov, the head of the government of Russia’s Dagestan Republic on the Caspian Sea, reported that three men travelling with an aid convoy from the republic had been killed and another hospitalised with an injury.

The convoy had been carrying aid to a Russian region on the border with Ukraine, he said. Melikov did not say where the incident occurred.
Russia, which marks the Orthodox Christmas on January 7, along with many Orthodox Christians in Ukraine, has rejected a ceasefire over the Christmas period.
An overnight Ukrainian drone attack set tanks containing oil products ablaze in the port city of Temryuk in Russia’s southern Krasnodar region, authorities in the city on the Sea of Azov reported on Thursday.
The blaze affected an area of 2,000 square metres in the city on the Taman Peninsula, they said. Dozens of fire service and civil protection officers were working to contain the fire.
Ukraine has been warding off a full-scale Russian invasion since February 2022.

data.title: Mercedes-Benz acquires stake in Chinese autonomous driving developer for US$191 million
data.description:
Mercedes-Benz on Thursday said it completed the acquisition of a 3 per cent stake, worth 1.34 billion yuan (US$191 million), in motorcycle maker-turned-autonomous driving systems developer Chongqing Qianli Technology, three months after the deal was first announced.
The transaction involved Mercedes-Benz Digital Technology, the German carmaker’s tech unit, and Shanghai-listed Lifan Holdings, which transferred 135.6 million shares of Qianli at 9.87 yuan per share.
Before the transaction, Lifan Holdings – once China’s top motorcycle maker – held more than 5 per cent of Qianli’s shares. After closing the deal, Mercedes-Benz became Qianli’s fifth largest stakeholder.
According to the terms of the deal, the transfer of the shares “will not lead to changes in the controlling shareholders and actual controllers of the company, and will not affect the offer for acquisition”.
Mercedes-Benz said it was committed to not reducing its shareholding in Qianli for at least 12 months.
The acquisition underscored efforts by Mercedes-Benz to deepen its ties and strategic collaborations in the world’s largest car market.

Mercedes-Benz, Tesla and Volvo last month became the first foreign carmakers approved to deploy artificial intelligence chatbots in their vehicles in China, which marked a potential acceleration of Beijing’s vetting process for AI services.
The Cyberspace Administration of China’s Beijing branch registered the “Mercedes-Benz virtual assistant” as a generative AI service at about the same time when the regulator’s Shanghai branch approved Tesla Shanghai’s xBot service and Volvo’s local chatbot Xiao Wo.
In the past few months, Mercedes-Benz has also invested in a number of China’s rising AI companies.
Founded in 1992 as a motorcycle manufacturer, Lifan Holdings later broadened its operations to include automobile components. Its investors included a private equity fund supported by Chinese carmaker Geely and the municipal government of Chongqing.
Lifan Holdings chairman Yin Qi co-founded and previously served as CEO at Chinese AI firm Megvii, which is backed by Alibaba Group Holding. Hangzhou-based Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.

data.title: Olympic snowboarding champion Su Yiming becomes first Chinese winner of Big Air World Cup
data.description:
Olympic snowboard champion Su Yiming has become China’s first overall winner of the FIS Snowboard Big Air World Cup and has also added another Guinness World Record to his name as he builds towards defending his Winter Games title at Milano Cortina 2026.
The 21-year-old secured the Crystal Globe trophy for the 2025-26 Big Air season after back-to-back World Cup victories at Genting Resort Secret Garden in Zhangjiakou and the Shougang Big Air Jump in Beijing, placing him top of the overall Big Air standings.
The Shougang Big Air Jump venue is where he won Big Air gold at the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics.
“I have been waiting for this for so long,” Su wrote on social media after clinching the title.
His success came as another recognition of his technical achievement was announced.

Su’s “back-to-back 1980” – two consecutive 1,980-degree rotations each with 5½ spins on a single run – has been officially certified as a world record by Guinness World Records.
“The most 1980-degree spins completed in a single coherent run in snowboarding,” according to a post on the organisation’s official Weibo account on Thursday.
The trick, completed during a training session at the Stamping Grounds Project in Saas-Fee, Switzerland, on October 7, is considered one of the most difficult manoeuvres in the sport.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by 苏翊鸣Su Yiming🦋 (@mingsuyi)



This is not Su’s first Guinness World Record. He was also recognised as the first person to complete a backslide 1980 Indy Crail in snowboarding, a trick he landed at Prime Park Sessions in Stubai, Austria, on October 28, 2021.
The move features an inward 1,980-degree spin combined with an Indy Crail grab, executed off a backward (backside) take-off, making it one of the most technical and demanding tricks in the discipline.
Su is also a former child actor, but he put his career on the silver screen on hold to focus on snowboarding. His most notable role was playing Little Shuan Zi in Hong Kong director Tsui Hark’s 2014 film The Taking of Tiger Mountain.

data.title: Exhibition traces China’s long links with Kenya, from Admiral Zheng He to belt and road
data.description:
More than six centuries ago, roughly 80 years ahead of Portuguese explorer Vasco da Gama, Chinese admiral Zheng He reached the eastern coast of Africa with a massive fleet and a crew of more than 20,000 under his command.
The Ming dynasty ships braved formidable waves and traversed vast distances on seven epic maritime expeditions to reach the shores of Southeast Asia, South Asia, West Asia and finally the eastern coast of Africa.
Despite his achievements, Zheng He remains largely absent from Kenya’s history curriculum. In an effort to raise awareness of these ancient links with China, Beijing is increasing funding for archaeology, DNA research and exhibitions.
By linking China’s ancient Maritime Silk Road with its modern Belt and Road Initiative, Beijing aims to highlight historical ties to contemporary projects, such as Kenya’s Standard Gauge Railway and Lamu port.
The latest example of these efforts is an exhibition at the National Museums of Kenya in the capital Nairobi, commemorating both the 620th anniversary of Zheng He’s voyages and 62 years of the modern relationship between the two countries.

Speaking at the exhibition, which opened on December 19, Chinese ambassador to Kenya Guo Haiyan said that historic Kenyan ports like Mombasa and Malindi were key stops for Zheng He, illustrating a long-standing presence based on harmony rather than force.
“What Zheng He’s fleet brought were not warships or colonial conquest but silk, porcelain, tea and advanced production technologies,” she said, contrasting this legacy with the European powers that arrived to conquer and exploit.
In return for these goods, the Chinese explorers secured spices, sandalwood and precious stones. “What they sought was not territory or wealth, but friendship, knowledge and dialogue,” Guo said.
Adding that a nation’s strength should be a positive force for development rather than plunder, Guo noted that differences between civilisations were not a source of conflict but a driving force for mutual enrichment.
Stephen Chan, from the School of Oriental and African Studies at the University of London, noted that Zheng He’s voyages were genuine, well-documented historical events that focused on exploration rather than colonialism.
Chan, a professor of world politics and international relations, said the admiral had even brought a live giraffe back to China to showcase the wonders of Africa to the emperor in what came to be known as “giraffe diplomacy”.
The giraffe – a gift to the Emperor Yongle from the Sultan of Malindi in 1414 – was celebrated by the Ming court as a qilin, a mythical Chinese creature regarded as a sacred omen of peace and prosperity.
Chan observed that the fleet caused no harm to local populations. “Remnants of Ming dynasty ceramics have been found on East African beaches. No disparaging words of African people have been found in any of the Chinese records of these voyages,” he added.
David Shinn, a China-Africa expert and professor at George Washington University’s Elliott School of International Affairs, also said the Chinese acted as navigators and merchants rather than conquerors.


Shinn noted that modern Chinese diplomacy made frequent reference to the Zheng He voyages, reinforcing a lengthy relationship, albeit one that was largely interrupted until the communist government’s support of African liberation groups in the late 1950s.
While da Gama’s visits to Africa occurred later than those of Zheng He, “European historians have been more effective at telling the story than Chinese historians,” Shinn said.
China’s maritime expansion ended in 1436, when Emperor Zhengtong prohibited the construction of overseas vessels and warships, he added.
“Chinese naval vessels did not return to African shores until 2000, when PLA Navy warships called in at Dar es Salaam in Tanzania and the Simon’s Town naval base near Cape Town [in South Africa].”

In her remarks at the Nairobi exhibition, Guo noted that oral traditions in Shanga and Siyu on Pate Island in the Lamu archipelago still recounted the story of a shipwreck of one of Zheng He’s vessels.
The surviving sailors are said to have killed a python that was terrorising the locals, who showed their gratitude by permitting them to settle and marry local women – a tradition supported by discoveries of ancient Chinese porcelain and the distinct physical features of some local families.
In 2002, DNA testing confirmed the Chinese ancestry of a family on the island, linking them to the 15th-century fleet and leading to a scholarship for direct descendant Mwamaka Sharifu, who studied traditional Chinese medicine in China.
According to Guo, the archaeological findings and oral histories are a testimony to the bond of kinship across the ocean. The ancient Maritime Silk Road pioneered by Zheng He had forged a lasting connection between the two peoples, she said.
Mary Gikungu, director general of the National Museums of Kenya, said the exhibition spoke directly to shared histories, intercultural encounters and peaceful exchanges across the Indian Ocean.
“Through photographs and virtual narratives, we are invited to rediscover connections that continue to shape our contemporary relations,” she said.

data.title: UBTech’s US$237 million Fenglong deal tightens humanoid robot supply chain
data.description:
Chinese humanoid robot maker UBTech Robotics is moving to lock down key manufacturing capacity, agreeing to pay nearly 1.7 billion yuan (US$237 million) in cash to take control of Shenzhen-listed mechanical component maker Zhejiang Fenglong as it pushes to scale up humanoid robot production.
The two-stage deal will begin with UBTech acquiring a 29.99 per cent stake in Fenglong from existing shareholders for 1.16 billion yuan, followed by a voluntary partial offer to buy a further 13.02 per cent for about 504 million yuan, according to a filing to the Hong Kong stock exchange on Wednesday.
The offer prices Fenglong’s shares at 17.72 yuan each, a 10 per cent discount to the company’s December 17 close of 19.68 yuan, before trading was halted.
After the first phase, UBTech will gain the right to nominate six of Fenglong’s seven board directors. Once both stages are completed, the Hong Kong-listed robotics firm expects to hold at least a 43 per cent stake in the company.
Fenglong manufactures engines for garden tools, automotive components and machine pressure-control systems. Its shares resumed trading on Thursday and immediately hit the 10 per cent daily limit, opening at 21.65 yuan.
UBTech, which listed in Hong Kong in December 2023, last traded at HK$109.5 during Wednesday’s half-day session, giving it a market capitalisation of HK$47.38 billion.
The acquisition is intended to leverage Fenglong’s manufacturing expertise and supply chain to accelerate UBTech’s humanoid robotics development, while also giving the company its first A-share-listed subsidiary, it said.
The move comes as Chinese robotics firms increasingly turn to acquisitions of mainland-listed companies to deepen industrial capabilities and secure access to domestic capital markets.
Unitree Robotics completed its pre-IPO tutoring process last month and is expected to file for an onshore listing soon.
Meanwhile, Tencent Holdings-backed AgiBot, also known as Zhiyuan Robotics, which this year took a controlling stake in Shanghai-listed Swancor Advanced Materials, was reportedly preparing for a Hong Kong listing in 2026.
Chongqing-based Sevnce Robotics, which focuses on emergency safety robots, is also set to acquire at least a 44.84 per cent stake in Shenzhen-listed LNG logistics provider Senton Energy, according to a recent exchange filing.

data.title: Architect of Hong Kong’s Octopus card retires with dreams of ‘tap-free’ technology
data.description:
Sammy Kam Chi-sum, the primary architect of Hong Kong’s contactless Octopus cards, is retiring after nearly 30 years with the company at the end of this month, before what he says is an inevitable transition to a technology that will not even require tapping for certain transactions.
Octopus’ chief operations officer began his journey in 1995 when he joined the MTR Corporation’s marketing team to help launch what would become the world’s first contactless system that revolutionised public transport and retail payments.
Now in his 60s, Kam said his vision was to create a “tap-free” commute environment by using ultra-wideband wireless technology with “centimetre-perfect” location tracking to enable passengers to move through entry and exit barriers without needing to tap a card on a sensor.
“I really hope that in the next two to three years, ultra-wideband can be fully realised. The system will sense your presence, deduct the fare [from your Octopus card] and open the gate for you automatically,” he said.
“Actually, the technology is already here. We are actively exploring it, but I will leave the rest for my successors to handle.”
Kam will be succeeded by Octopus’ head of transport business, Nora Tang Chui-shan, and the general manager of the company’s technical department Tracy Poon Suet-wing on New Year’s Day.
The MTR Corporation has a 64.02 per cent stake in Octopus, while the rest is owned by the Kowloon-Canton Railway Corporation, KMB, Citybus and Sun Ferry.
As he looked back on his time at Octopus, Kam did not shy away from some of the company’s less savoury moments.
In 2010, it sold the personal data of its nearly 2 million users to third-party entities for marketing, earning HK$44 million (US$5.7 million) – a scandal that Kam said he still recalled “vividly”.
Following initial denials and a subsequent public inquiry, Octopus’ CEO at the time, Prudence Chan Bik-wah, resigned under immense pressure.
Chastened, Octopus pledged to pay closer attention to corporate social responsibility by donating its data profits to charity the following year.

There were other missteps, Kam admits, but they arose from technical flaws and carelessness rather than corporate misgovernance. During its early phase, Octopus cards were deactivated after six months of inactivity. Once, when a bus passenger used an expired card, the reader beeped with the message, “Illegal usage”.
“The bus driver didn’t know how to handle it, so he called the company and was told to take the passenger to the police station. It was a very unfortunate incident. I still remember it clearly today, and I still feel I owe an apology to that passenger,” Kam said.
Also retiring after a 17-year tenure is sales and marketing director Rita Li Yuk-yi, who looked back on the high-stakes negotiations that led to the integration of Octopus into Samsung Pay in 2017 and Apple Pay in 2020.
“We thought it was very hard to talk to a global handset manufacturer because we were a local payment [system], not a global payment service like Mastercard or Visa,” she said.
“But I met a contact person and asked about lining up with Samsung. That was how we truly began a dialogue and even went to Samsung’s headquarters in [South] Korea to talk to them and pitch the whole thing.”
Li, who is in her 60s, also highlighted the intensity of working with Apple, which had very high standards.
“We learned a lot from them. Whether it was technical or commercial, [Apple] was very demanding, especially regarding loading speed and stability,” she said.
Kam and Li navigated the competitive landscape of the government’s consumption voucher scheme, which was launched during the Covid-19 pandemic in phases.
The company rolled out over HK$250 million in cash rewards and welcome offers to maintain its dominance over rival stored-value platforms.
“We brainstormed many strategies and had to think carefully about how to roll everything out, how to launch [offers] and when. Besides getting customers, we also had to figure out how to incentivise them to actually use [the vouchers],” Li said.
“It was actually a very challenging project, but I’m also very happy because the team managed to gain the most support from the public in using their consumption vouchers with us.”

As they approached retirement, both leaders said they looked towards “paying it forward”, with Li planning to dedicate her time to mentoring teenagers.
She will be succeeded by the company’s retail business and partnership general manager, Edwin Lai Ka-chung.
Kam said he wanted to contribute to the development of the city’s innovation and technology sector.
“I have experience and knowledge to help others do things that are useful to society or help people, it would be a way to give back,” he said.
“I’m very willing to participate in being an incubator or adviser to help local start-ups and promote Hong Kong’s innovation and technology development.”

data.title: With 1MDB verdict due, will Malaysia’s Najib have to ‘prepare for the worst’?
data.description:
Malaysia’s High Court is poised on Friday to deliver its verdict in the final criminal trial of jailed former prime minister Najib Razak, bringing to a close one of Southeast Asia’s most far-reaching corruption sagas.
The case centres on allegations that the disgraced 72-year-old misappropriated 2.3 billion ringgit (US$490 million) from the scandal-ridden Malaysian sovereign wealth fund 1Malaysia Development Berhad (1MDB). Najib is currently in Kajang Prison, over halfway through a sentence reduced to six years for a conviction in a separate case linked to a former 1MDB subsidiary.
Prosecutors allege the money was siphoned off through a complex network of banks, shell companies and intermediaries while Najib was both prime minister and finance chief.
The High Court in Kuala Lumpur is expected to rule on four counts of abuse of power and 21 charges of money laundering. If Najib is found guilty, he could face lengthy prison terms that could run into decades, depending on whether any sentences are ordered to be served consecutively.
The decision will be delivered at the Palace of Justice in Putrajaya and will be closely watched by a Malaysian public split over its opinion towards Najib, who is adored by his loyalists and loathed by critics for the corruption that billowed out under his administration.
It will be delivered by Justice Collin Lawrence Sequerah, who presided over the trial and has since been elevated to the Federal Court.

A case years in the making
The trial is the last unresolved criminal proceeding against Najib linked to 1MDB, the scandal that erupted in 2015 and ultimately contributed to the collapse of his government three years later.
The trial stretched over seven years, with prosecutors calling 50 witnesses across 235 days, while the defence presented 26 witnesses, including Najib himself.
Prosecutors sought to prove that Najib was not an unwitting bystander, misled by aides or financiers – as his defence claimed – but was, in fact, the central decision-maker at 1MDB who controlled its affairs and benefited from its funds. They alleged the money moved through Najib’s personal accounts at AmIslamic Bank in Kuala Lumpur between 2011 and 2014.
Najib’s defence team, led by senior lawyer Muhammad Shafee Abdullah, has consistently denied his client’s wrongdoing.
The defence argued that the funds were legitimate political donations from Saudi Arabia and that Najib was deceived by shadowy fugitive financier Low Taek Jho, better known as Jho Low, whom Shafee described as the “main architect” of the plundering scheme.
Low remains at large and is wanted by authorities in Malaysia, the United States and Singapore.

Global fallout
Malaysia’s taxpayers lost billions of dollars to the 1MDB scandal. But it has also triggered investigations and prosecutions across multiple jurisdictions, including the US, Luxembourg, Switzerland and Singapore, in what analysts say is one of the most egregious examples of kleptocracy in the modern era.
US prosecutors previously described the affair as one of the largest corruption cases ever pursued by the Department of Justice, alleging that more than US$4.5 billion was looted from the fund and pushed through banks and assets across the world.
Stolen money was traced to luxury real estate in New York and Los Angeles, a superyacht once owned by Low, and works by artists including Monet and Van Gogh.
Hollywood was also pulled into the scandal, with funds linked to 1MDB used to finance The Wolf of Wall Street, starring Leonardo DiCaprio, who later returned artworks and other gifts tied to the case after US authorities launched asset recovery proceedings.
Former Goldman Sachs banker Roger Ng Chong Hwa was convicted in the US in April 2022 over his role in helping raise funds for 1MDB.
He was later extradited to Malaysia in October 2023 to assist local investigations, following the return in July that year of former 1MDB lawyer and Jho Low associate Jasmine Loo Ai Swan after years on the run.
The Malaysian government in October said it had so far recovered about 20.73 billion ringgit through settlements with financial institutions linked to the scandal.
This includes US$3.9 billion from a settlement with Goldman Sachs, comprising US$2.5 billion in cash and a US$1.4 billion asset recovery guarantee, as well as 2.83 billion ringgit from AmBank Group and US$330 million from JPMorgan (Switzerland).

High stakes
Najib has been incarcerated at Kajang Prison since August 2022 after being convicted in a separate case involving SRC International, a former 1MDB subsidiary.
That conviction, which involved 42 million ringgit, initially carried a 12-year jail term that was later reduced to six years following partial royal clemency. He has been tirelessly pushing the courts to allow him to serve his time under house arrest rather than in jail.
But earlier this week, the High Court rejected Najib’s bid to serve the remainder of that sentence under house arrest, ruling that an alleged royal addendum permitting such an arrangement had no legal effect.
It remains unclear whether Najib would remain in prison immediately if convicted in the 1MDB case, though his lawyers have said they will seek a stay of execution pending appeal, potentially taking the case to the Court of Appeal and the Federal Court.
Political analyst Oh Ei Sun said the latest developments had reinforced expectations that Najib faced an uphill battle in court.
“Well, as with all indicted, he has to prepare for the worst,” Oh told This Week in Asia.

data.title: Maximalist cocktails are back as bartenders go beyond garish to create ‘something special’
data.description:
After years of minimalist, pared-back cocktails, maximalism is back.
Drinkers are looking for more than just a simple serve, and bartenders are dreaming up bold colour palettes, layered flavours, oversized garnishes, theatrical glassware and playful twists on classic drinks.
The trend harks back to the “really out there” drinks service of the ’90s, says Hannah Sharman-Cox, co-founder of The Pinnacle Guide, which rates cocktail bars around the world with a “pin” system.
“It’s a little bit like that era’s grown-up cousin has taken the reins – more decadent luxury rather than garish waste,” says Sharman-Cox.
“Even the simple martini is starting to get more elaborate garnishes – big pickles, colourful cornichons – and we’re here for it,” she adds.

Mason Park, the general manager at Alice Cheongdam, in Seoul, South Korea, agrees that today’s maximalism takes a more thoughtful approach.
In the past, it was about flashiness and “prop” garnishes. The focus now, he says, is on the flavour impact, as well as sight, touch and smell.
There is also thought given to environmental sustainability, according to Siobhan Payne, The Pinnacle Guide’s other co-founder.
“Garnishes are increasingly designed to be edible or reusable, so we’re seeing more elaborate partnerships between the bar and the kitchen to create something delicious that truly complements the cocktail,” she says.
For Park, creative, attention-grabbing drinks also fuel and sustain the art of mixology.
“We work with so many elaborate elements and get so many ‘wow’ reactions from customers when we serve them, we believe that this maximalism – making these fun cocktails – actually plays a role in sustaining the profession of bartending itself,” he says.

Especially at a time when people are more cautious with their money, the industry needs to level up to give them a reason to visit bars – “not just for the simple experience of enjoying the night, but also to get something completely different”, says Emanuele Pedrazzani, head bartender at Nightjar, an underground bar in London.
At Alice Cheongdam, which takes its inspiration from Lewis Carroll’s Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland, the signature cocktail is Foggy Fongo, a riff on the book’s famous depiction of a caterpillar smoking a pipe on top of a mushroom.
Served in a smoky, mushroom-shaped glass, the cocktail is built on bourbon, frankincense and palo santo (a Peruvian wood), with macadamia and hazelnut, as well as artichoke, sherry and the prized pine mushroom.
“We extract the aroma from pine mushrooms using glycerine, and then we infuse that mushroom fragrance into the cocktail as smoke,” Park says.
The cocktail also features a truffle cookie, which can be accessed by opening the 3D printed mushroom-shaped cap.
“You can think of it as a cocktail that offers an experience where you eat and drink together,” Park says.
On the other side of the world, Nightjar has the elaborately presented Beyond the Sea cocktail, served in a giant shell. It is loosely inspired by the Salty Dog (gin or vodka and grapefruit juice with a salty rim), with a combination of gin, fino sherry with kombu seaweed, shiso leaves, limoncello, absinthe and grapefruit, finished off with a briny foam to evoke sea spray, and an olive.
It is an immersive experience, and it is hard to know where to start.
“From one of the narrow ends!” Pedrazzani quips. He says he loves to make customers laugh – “to create some sort of connection” – and he jokes that in this case, sometimes they do not listen and the drink ends up all over them.
At the Bon Vivants craft cocktail bar in Nassau, the Bahamas, experience is everything.
If you order the Mama Maggie, the fruity mix of coconut rum, passion fruit liqueur, hibiscus, mango, lime and orange juice will be delivered to you in a photogenic porcelain pig topped with colourful fresh flowers.

“When it comes to these types of drinks, customers want something they can post,” says Niko Imbert, Bon Vivants’ senior vice-president of hospitality.
They also want to see you put care and effort into the product, he adds.
“It’s just like a culinary experience in my opinion. If I see someone taking their time to create something special for me, I’m very thankful for it.”
To up your maximalist cocktail game at home, Imbert says, consider using vintage and unusual glassware, bar tools, garnishing, glitter or even a cocktail smoker.
“Just don’t go overboard, right?” says Jose Maria Donde, beverage manager and head mixologist at The Bedford Stone Street in New York.
He creates a theatrical take on the Paloma, the Rey Bucanero (Spanish for “King Buccaneer”). Alongside the traditional tequila and grapefruit, it contains rum, elderflower, cacao liqueur and yuzu, and is garnished with shiso leaf “waves” and an orchid flower.
At home, Donde suggests, start with something “that’s going to make you feel good and looks nice”, and add to it later as needed.

data.title: China sharpens sales pitch for overseas investment with new list of sought-after sectors
data.description:
China is taking steps to reshape the direction of its foreign investment, unveiling a revised catalogue of encouraged industries that steers overseas capital towards the advanced and technologically complex sectors Beijing’s policymakers view as future growth drivers.
The revised catalogue, which takes effect in February 2026, covers 1,679 industries – 205 more than the current version, which was released in 2022.
In a statement on Wednesday, the Ministry of Commerce said the changes are aimed at “strengthening China’s appeal to foreign investors, improving support services for foreign-funded firms and creating a more transparent, stable and predictable business environment”.
The revised catalogue widens the scope of incentives for overseas investment in advanced manufacturing, adding new fields to its coverage umbrella. Among others, these include nucleic acid drugs, high-end medical equipment, intelligent testing instruments, smart energy systems and deep-sea robots.
Yuan Qian, a lead researcher at the Chinese Academy of Macroeconomic Research, was quoted by state broadcaster CCTV as saying the catalogue is a core policy tool for promoting foreign investment and optimising the country’s industrial mix.
He said the catalogue helps China “better counter an adverse external environment by offsetting external uncertainty with the certainty of its own development, while providing global investors with clear, stable and transparent investment expectations”.
Greater emphasis has also been placed on modern services, with high-end shipping, virtual power plants, pet healthcare, sports tourism, camping, homestays, property services, internet-enabled healthcare and other sectors added to the catalogue.


At the same time, the revision sets up avenues for more foreign inflows in certain regions, adding sectors like cruise tourism, ice-and-snow equipment, marine ecological restoration, computing infrastructure and renewable energy projects. These fields, and others new to the catalogue, are concentrated in central China, as well as the country's west and northeast.
The revised catalogue is being published amid a campaign by Beijing to coax back foreign investment, as the massive inflows that once aided China's growth and development have slowed considerably in recent years.
In 2024, the world’s second-largest economy attracted 826.2 billion yuan (US$116.2 billion) of foreign investment, down 27.1 per cent year on year and dropping 32.9 per cent from the peak of 1.23 trillion yuan recorded in 2022.
The slide has continued this year. China’s total foreign investment over the first 11 months of 2025 reached 693.2 billion yuan, down 7.5 per cent compared with the same period last year.
To implement the new edition of the catalogue, the commerce ministry said it will step up policy communication, refine supporting measures and strengthen service guarantees to ensure foreign firms fully benefit from the revised framework.
In its statement, the ministry said it would “proactively coordinate and resolve difficulties and issues faced by foreign-invested enterprises during policy implementation.”
Foreign-invested projects listed in the catalogue are eligible for major policy incentives, the ministry said, including tariff exemptions on equipment imported specifically for project use, preferential treatment in land supply and discounted land prices for intensive industrial projects.
They will also have access to a reduced 15 per cent corporate income tax rate in the country's western regions, as well as the southern island province of Hainan; additionally, tax credits will be available for overseas investors who reinvest distributed profits into encouraged sectors.
